questions.optionB: Blue-green pigment (pyocyanin), grape-like odor, oxidase positive, and intrinsic multidrug resistance
questions.explanation: P. aeruginosa: Gram-negative, obligate aerobe, non-lactose fermenter. Produces: pyocyanin (blue-green pigment), pyoverdin (yellow-green fluorescent siderophore). Grape-like/fruity odor. Oxidase positive. Intrinsically resistant to many antibiotics. Causes: burn wound infections, CF lung infections (biofilm, mucoid strains), nosocomial pneumonia (VAP), UTI (catheter), otitis externa ('swimmer's ear'), hot-tub folliculitis, ecthyma gangrenosum (immunocompromised). Treatment: anti-pseudomonal (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, meropenem, ciprofloxacin). Reference: Ananthanarayan — Pseudomonas.

questions.questionText: E. coli is the most common cause of:
questions.optionB: Urinary tract infections (UTI), Gram-negative sepsis, and neonatal meningitis (K1 capsule)
questions.explanation: E. coli: Gram-negative, lactose-fermenting, facultative anaerobe. Most common cause of: UTI (#1 overall — pyelonephritis and cystitis, P-fimbriae/pili), Gram-negative bacteremia/sepsis, neonatal meningitis (K1 capsular antigen). Diarrheagenic strains: ETEC (traveler's diarrhea — heat-labile/heat-stable toxins), EHEC/O157:H7 (bloody diarrhea → HUS — Shiga-like toxin), EIEC (dysentery), EPEC (infant diarrhea), EAEC (persistent diarrhea). Grows on MacConkey agar (pink lactose fermenter). Reference: Ananthanarayan — E. coli.

questions.optionB: Cholera toxin (AB subunit) — activates adenylyl cyclase → increased cAMP → massive secretory diarrhea (rice-water stools)
questions.explanation: V. cholerae: Gram-negative curved rod, comma-shaped, oxidase positive. O1 (El Tor biotype) and O139: epidemic strains. Cholera toxin: AB5 structure. B subunit: binds GM1 ganglioside on intestinal epithelium. A subunit: ADP-ribosylates Gs protein → permanently activates adenylyl cyclase → increased cAMP → Cl- secretion + Na+/H2O loss → massive watery diarrhea ('rice-water stools'). NO tissue invasion or inflammation. Treatment: ORS (most important), doxycycline/azithromycin. TCBS agar: selective medium. Reference: Ananthanarayan — V. cholerae.

questions.optionB: Peptic ulcer disease, gastric adenocarcinoma, and MALT lymphoma
questions.explanation: H. pylori: Gram-negative spiral/curved rod, microaerophilic, urease positive (key feature — basis for CLO/rapid urease test and urea breath test). Colonizes gastric mucosa (antrum). Virulence: urease (neutralizes acid), CagA (oncogenic), VacA (vacuolating cytotoxin). Associations: chronic gastritis (type B), duodenal/gastric ulcers, gastric adenocarcinoma (Class I carcinogen — WHO), MALToma. Triple therapy: PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin × 14 days. Nobel Prize 2005 (Marshall & Warren). Reference: Ananthanarayan — H. pylori.

questions.questionText: Neisseria meningitidis is an important cause of meningitis and is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Gram-negative kidney-shaped diplococci, oxidase positive, polysaccharide capsule, and causes Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome
questions.explanation: N. meningitidis: Gram-negative kidney-shaped (bean-shaped) diplococci, oxidase positive, grows on chocolate agar/Thayer-Martin (selective). Polysaccharide capsule (13 serogroups, A/B/C/W/Y most important). Causes: meningitis (petechial rash, CSF: PMN, low glucose, high protein), meningococcemia, Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage → adrenal insufficiency → shock → death). Droplet transmission. Chemoprophylaxis: rifampin/ciprofloxacin for close contacts. Vaccines: quadrivalent (A/C/W/Y), serogroup B vaccine (MenB). Reference: Ananthanarayan — N. meningitidis.
